title: GaN-on-SiC Foundry Slots Are Getting Harder to Book — What That Means for Your Next T/R Module
gist: GaN-on-SiC foundry capacity for 2026 has tightened structurally due to a February 2026 DoD emergency mandate prioritizing military GaN over commercial 5G, plus NVIDIA's planned shift to SiC interposers in Rubin redirecting substrate supply. Pricing is bifurcating: Chinese 6-inch wafers at $400-500 versus Western premium wafers at $1,000-2,000, while the 8-inch transition leaves 2026 in an awkward capacity gap.

insights.claim: In February 2026, amid Middle East conflict prioritizing electronic warfare and anti-drone defense, the US DoD invoked emergency procurement powers to legally mandate that top-tier domestic foundries prioritize military GaN orders over commercial 5G and consumer electronics.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Commercial and non-US dual-use radar programs face immediate foundry slot scarcity; reserve Western capacity now or face premium pricing.
insights.quote: the US Department of Defense invoked emergency procurement powers and legally mandated that top-tier domestic semiconductor foundries prioritize military GaN orders over commercial 5G and consumer electronics applications.
insights.locator: In February 2026, as conflict in the Middle East

insights.claim: NVIDIA is reportedly planning to replace traditional silicon interposers with silicon carbide in its next-generation Rubin architecture, potentially redirecting SiC substrate capacity toward data centers and compressing availability for RF device makers sharing the same upstream supply chain.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: A new AI-driven demand stream competes for SiC substrate, structurally tightening RF foundry supply; watch NVIDIA Rubin sourcing announcements.
insights.quote: NVIDIA is reportedly planning to replace traditional silicon interposers with silicon carbide in its next-generation Rubin architecture
insights.locator: Simultaneously, a second demand stream has emerged

insights.claim: Rapid SiC capacity expansion from Chinese players caused roughly a 30% drop in SiC wafer prices in 2024, with entrants like Tankeblue and Guangdong TySiC offering 6-inch wafers at USD 400-500 by leveraging subsidized utilities.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Chinese price erosion forces Western incumbents to compete on defect density and support, not price; track substrate qualification status for defense use.
insights.quote: approximately a 30% drop in SiC wafer prices in 2024

insights.claim: Premium Western GaN-on-SiC wafers run USD 1,000 to USD 2,000 per unit, and DoD prioritization means remaining non-reserved capacity at US and allied fabs is priced at a premium, raising effective cost per wafer slot for teams without a long-term supply agreement.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Effective Western foundry costs are rising, not falling, despite headline Chinese price drops; build provenance constraints into BOM models early.
insights.quote: high wafer costs, often USD 1,000 to USD 2,000 per unit, continue to slow adoption among cost-sensitive manufacturers

insights.claim: Low-cost Chinese substrates are not yet qualified for most defense radar applications, and GaN/SiC yield rates remain lower than silicon, so a 5-10% yield drop on an expensive wafer run is a material cost event at smaller volumes.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Provenance and yield constraints prevent simple cost arbitrage to Chinese wafers for defense programs.
insights.locator: The bifurcation matters for T/R module designers

insights.claim: Wolfspeed demonstrated a 300mm GaN-on-SiC boule in January 2026 and SK Siltron plans 30,000 eight-inch wafers per month in Michigan by late 2026, but qualification inertia leaves 2026 in an awkward period where 6-inch capacity tightens and 8-inch is not yet broadly available for production.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Programs with 12-18 month tape-out windows face a real scheduling and budgeting gap; the 8-inch transition is not yet a bridge.
insights.quote: leaving 2026 in an awkward middle period where 6-inch capacity is tightening and 8-inch is not yet broadly available for production runs.
insights.locator: The 8-inch transition is creating a gap

insights.claim: As fabrication yields improve on 8-inch wafers, a 15% price drop in GaN power units is anticipated.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Cost relief is coming but lags 2026 program needs; long-dated designs benefit more than near-term tape-outs.
insights.quote: a 15% price drop in GaN power units is anticipated
insights.locator: as fabrication yields improve on 8-inch wafers

insights.claim: Foundries with the most relevant GaN-on-SiC processes for radar FEM and HPA applications (e.g. WIN Semiconductors' 250nm and 120nm) are operating closer to capacity utilization than their public communications suggest, so capacity reservations should be initiated before the pre-production stage.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Treat foundry slot access as a gating program risk, not a procurement afterthought.
insights.quote: The foundries with the most relevant processes for radar FEM and HPA applications are operating closer to capacity utilization than their public communications suggest.
insights.locator: First, if your T/R module design depends

insights.claim: Licensing production-ready GaN IP with existing foundry tape-out data and an established process relationship reduces foundry slot negotiation from a zero-baseline conversation to a capacity expansion discussion.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: IP provenance is a strategic lever for foundry access; relevant to fabless RF IP sourcing decisions.
insights.locator: Third, for teams evaluating whether to license
